messages.content:
Referring to a source is not required for collectively written sources.

Is this correct?


		
No.

		
Yes.

		
No, if a source is created by the contribution of fewer than 100 people, as is the case with a book that consists of chapters written by different authors

		
Yes, if a source is created by the contribution of more than 100 people, as is the case with Wikipedia.
No, if a source is created collectively, it is still necessary to refer to it and provide proper citations to acknowledge the contributions of the authors or contributors.

You found an interesting quote from Lindlof (1995) in the work of Hart (2013).

Which way of referring to this source is not correct?



		
You look for the original source and find that source. You take over the quote literally:
"In qualitative research, one interviews people to understand their perspective [...], or to analyze certain kinds of discourse" (Lindlof, 1995, p. 5).

		
You quote indirectly: Lindlof (1995, as quoted in Hart, 2013, p. 480) explains that in qualitative research people are interviewed to understand their perspective ..., or to analyse certain forms of conversation.

		
You quote and refer to Lindlof without looking up the book.
"In qualitative research, one interviews people to understand their perspective [...], or to analyze certain kinds of discourse.” (Lindlof, 1995, p. 5)

		
You take over the quote from Lindlof and refer to the encyclopedia:
According to Lindlof (1995, as quoted in Hart, 2013, p. 480): "in qualitative research, one interviews people to understand their perspective [...], or to analyze certain kinds of discourse”.

You take over the quote from Lindlof and refer to the encyclopedia:
According to Lindlof (1995, as quoted in Hart, 2013, p. 480): "in qualitative research, one interviews people to understand their perspective [...], or to analyze certain kinds of discourse”.

If you refer in the same sentence, in parentheses, to several publications by different authors, the ranking of those sources will take place:


		
chronologically on publication date. The different sources are separated by a comma.

		
alphabetically by name of the author(s). The different sources are separated by a comma.

		
alphabetically by name of the author(s). The different sources are separated by a semicolon.

		
chronologically on publication date. The different sources are separated by a semicolon.
alphabetically by name of the author(s). The different sources are separated by a semicolon.

n a paragraph you use different sources. In the first sentence you use information from source A, in the second sentence you use information from source B and in the last sentence of the paragraph you want to refer to source A again.

Which way of referring is correct?


		
In sentence 1 you refer to source A, in sentence 2 to source B and in the last sentence to source A.

		
In sentence 2 you refer to source B and in the last sentence to source A.

		
In sentence 1 you refer to source A and B.

		
In sentence 1 you refer to source A and in sentence 2 to source B.
In sentence 1 you refer to source A and in sentence 2 to source B.

A tweet contains information that you want to use in your paper.

Which statement about tweets is correct?


		
You have to include the reference data of the tweet in the text, because it is a source. Whether the source is subjective or objective, does not matter.

		
You have to include the reference data of the tweet in the text, because it is a written source.

		
You cannot mention tweets in a paper.

		
You do not have to mention the reference data of the tweet in the text, because Twitter is a subjective source.
You have to include the reference data of the tweet in the text, because it is a written source.

Below are several statements about referencing.

Which one is correct? 

 

		
If there is no author listed for a source, there is no need to refer to it.



		
If you refer to facts known to everyone (including people who are not part of the target audience) - such as water boiling at 100 degrees Celsius - you do not need to mention a source.



		
If you hear something from a teacher or on TV, you should not to refer to these kind of sources, because they are not written sources.



		
If you use sources that are not academic literature, you should not refer to them, because the material has not gone through a peer-review process.
If you hear something from a teacher or on TV, you should not refer to these kinds of sources because they are not written sources.

To base (so not literally copying) a complete page in your text on the same source is ...


		
no plagiarism if that source is an article from a peer-reviewed magazine.



		
no plagiarism if you refer correctly to this source, but an unacceptable way of working.



		
no plagiarism if you correctly refer to this source, and an acceptable method.



		
plagiarism, regardless of whether or not you refer correctly to this source.
no plagiarism if you correctly refer to this source, and an acceptable method.

You find an interesting article in a language you master, but which is different from the language in which you write your work. A paragraph of the article is suitable for your own paper.

Which action is not allowed?


		
You literally quote the paragraph in the original language, using quotation marks to indicate that it is a quote, and you refer to the original article.


		
You refer to the original article and give the content of the paragraph in your own words, making it clear how the content fits within your paper.


		
You translate the paragraph and use it in your own paper. It is not a literal translation, so you do not have to refer to the original article.


		
You give a literally translated version, preceded by 'literally translated', and correctly refer to the original article.
You give a literally translated version, preceded by 'literally translated', and correctly refer to the original article.

For which of the examples do you not need to mention the source?


		
When I formulate my own idea.



		
When I use information from the slides of lessons.



		
When I have found data from a published survey and pour it into a table.



		
When I paraphrase in my own words an idea from another author.
When I formulate my own idea.

Referring to a source is not required for collectively written sources.

Is this correct?


		
No.

		
Yes.

		
No, if a source is created by the contribution of fewer than 100 people, as is the case with a book that consists of chapters written by different authors

		
Yes, if a source is created by the contribution of more than 100 people, as is the case with Wikipedia.
No, if a source is created collectively, it is still necessary to refer to it and provide proper citations to acknowledge the contributions of the authors or contributors.

According to Lindlof (1995, as quoted in Hart, 2013, p. 480): "in qualitative research, one interviews people to understand their perspective [...], or to analyze certain kinds of discourse”.

You do not have to mention the reference data of the tweet in the text, because Twitter is a subjective source.

You find an interesting article in a language you master, but which is different from the language in which you write your work. A paragraph of the article is suitable for your own paper.

Which action is not allowed?


		
You literally quote the paragraph in the original language, using quotation marks to indicate that it is a quote, and you refer to the original article.


		
You refer to the original article and give the content of the paragraph in your own words, making it clear how the content fits within your paper.


		
You translate the paragraph and use it in your own paper. It is not a literal translation, so you do not have to refer to the original article.


		
You give a literally translated version, preceded by 'literally translated', and correctly refer to the original article.
You give a literally translated version, preceded by 'literally translated', and correctly refer to the original article.

your work. A paragraph of the article is suitable for your own paper.

Which action is not allowed?


		
You literally quote the paragraph in the original language, using quotation marks to indicate that it is a quote, and you refer to the original article.


		
You refer to the original article and give the content of the paragraph in your own words, making it clear how the content fits within your paper.

Referring to a source is not required for collectively written sources.

Is this correct?


		
No.

		
Yes.

		
No, if a source is created by the contribution of fewer than 100 people, as is the case with a book that consists of chapters written by different authors

		
Yes, if a source is created by the contribution of more than 100 people, as is the case with Wikipedia.
No.

If you have several sources that are written by the same author, in the same year.

Which of the following statements is true?


		
The reference list must include a letter (a, b, c, ...) after the date of publication, in alphabetical order of the title of those sources. The reference list does not contain those letters.

		
A letter (a, b, c, ...) should be included after the date of publication in the order in which the sources appear in the text. The reference list must also contain those letters.

		
The reference list must include a letter (a, b, c, ...) after the date of publication, in alphabetical order of the title of those sources. The reference list must also contain those letters.

		
A letters (a, b, c, ...) should appear after the date of publication in the order in which the sources appear in the text. The reference list does not contain those letters.
The reference list must include a letter (a, b, c, ...) after the date of publication, in alphabetical order of the title of those sources. The reference list must also contain those letters.

The complete reference of a source is: Ashton, R. H., Graul, P. R., & Newton, P. R. (1989). Audit delay and the timeliness of corporate reporting. Contemporary Accounting Research, 5(2), 657-673.

Which statement is correct:


		
All references to this source should be: (Ashton, Graul & Newton, 1989).

		
A first reference to this source is: (Ashton et al., 1989). As from the second reference to this source you use: (Ashton, Graul & Newton, 1989).

		
When you first refer to this source, you use: (Ashton, Graul & Newton, 1989). As from the second reference to this source you use: (Ashton et al., 1989).

		
All references to this source should be: (Ashton et al., 1989).
A first reference to this source is: (Ashton et al., 1989). As from the second reference to this source, you use: (Ashton et al., 1989).

A paragraph in your text is based on one source.

Where do you mention the reference data of that one source?



		
In the first sentence of that paragraph.

		
In the last sentence of that paragraph.

		
You may not base an entire paragraph on a single source.

		
No matter where.
In the first sentence of that paragraph.

Proper paraphrasing is ...

 

		
putting someone else's text in quotation marks and mentioning the source.

		
writing someone else's text in your own words and mentioning the source.

		
using an idea from someone else's text, in a completely different way and not mentioning the source.

		
changing a few words in someone else's text, so that the text becomes more 'yours' and mentioning the source.
Writing someone else's text in your own words and mentioning the source.

Can you use information from social media in a paper?

 

		
No, because social media platforms contain almost exclusively subjective information.

		
Yes, but only if that information is directly related to the subject of your paper, if you refer to it correctly and if do not violate anyone’s privacy.

		
No, because there are no rules about referring to social media platforms.

		
No, because social media information cannot be added value in an academic paper.
Yes, but only if that information is directly related to the subject of your paper, if you refer to it correctly, and if you do not violate anyone's privacy.

Reusing a piece of work that you have written yourself for another course unit or other assignment is considered to be ...


		
self-plagiarism.



		
no plagiarism, but it is an unacceptable way of working.



		
no plagiarism, if you passed that course or that assignment.



		
an acceptable method of working.
self-plagiarism.

Copying the work of a fellow student is considered to be ...


		
no plagiarism if the student has already left university.



		
an acceptable method of working.



		
plagiarism.



		
no plagiarism, but it is an unacceptable way of working.
plagiarism.

taking note of some of the ideas from an article and then forgetting to refer to the article when creating your own paper, is considered to be ...


		
no plagiarism, but only if you can demonstrate by means of an otherwise plagiarism-free paper that it really was a matter of oblivion.



		
no plagiarism, but it is an unacceptable way of working.



		
plagiarism.



		
an acceptable method of working.
plagiarism.

You have read a good review article about a certain topic. You decide to only use the information from this article as background information in the introduction to your project.

You do not refer to this article.



		
This is plagiarism, but not because you did not refer to the review article, but because you did not refer to the original articles on which the review article is based.



		
This is plagiarism, because you have to refer to all your sources, regardless of the place this information gets in your paper.



		
This is not plagiarism, because you do not use the information to counter or confirm your own ideas, it merely serves as background information.



		
This is not plagiarism, because if it has already been published in a review article, it is generally accepted knowledge.
This is not plagiarism, because you do not use the information to counter or confirm your own ideas, it merely serves as background information.
